perturbation.original_text:
8.1 Each party undertakes within three business days of the receipt of a written request of the other party or on termination of this Agreement, at the option of the other party:
(a) to return to the other party all of the other party's Confidential Information and that part of all documents and other material in its po ssession, custody or control that bear or incorporate any pa rt of the other pa rty's Confidential Information; or
(b) to destroy by shredding or incineration all of the other pa rty's Confidential Information and that part of all documents and other material in its possession, custody or control which bear or inco rporate any part of the other party's Confidential Information and take reason able steps to expung e all Confidential Information (and any copy that may have been ma de) from any computer, word processor or other device containing the Confidential Information. Any destruction of Confidential Information will be certified in writing to the other party by an authorised officer supervising it.
perturbation.changed_text:
8.1 Each party undertakes within 90 business days of the receipt of a written request of the other party or on termination of this Agreement, at the option of the receiving party:
(a) to return to the other party all of the other party's Confidential Information and that part of all documents and other material in its po ssession, custody or control that bear or incorporate any pa rt of the other pa rty's Confidential Information; or
(b) to destroy by shredding or incineration all of the other pa rty's Confidential Information and that part of all documents and other material in its possession, custody or control which bear or inco rporate any part of the other party's Confidential Information and take reason able steps to expung e all Confidential Information (and any copy that may have been ma de) from any computer, word processor or other device containing the Confidential Information. Any destruction of Confidential Information will be certified in writing to the other party by an authorised officer supervising it.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return or destruction timeframe from 3 business days to 90 business days significantly delays the process and favors the receiving party, potentially conflicting with obligations to protect trade secrets in a timely manner under intellectual property law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 4 of the EU Trade Secrets Directive requires member states to ensure that competent judicial authorities can order measures to prevent the use or disclosure of trade secrets unlawfully. A 90-day delay in returning or destroying confidential information could be considered an unreasonably long period, potentially prejudicing the trade secret holder's rights and hindering effective legal remedies against misappropriation, as the delay could exacerbate the damage caused by the misappropriation, especially if the disclosing party is located within the EU.

perturbation.justification: The change from 3 to 90 business days for returning or destroying confidential information directly contradicts the EU Trade Secrets Directive's intent to ensure timely measures against unlawful use or disclosure of trade secrets. While the snippet provided doesn't explicitly state a timeframe, the Law Explanation indicates a 90-day delay could prejudice the trade secret holder's rights, hindering effective legal remedies. The original text ensured quicker compliance, whereas the modified text introduces a significant delay that undermines the protection intended by the directive, making the terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 11.1 A notice or other communication under or in connection with th is Agreement shall be in writing, in Eng lish and shall be delive red personally or sent by fax, to the party due to receive the no tice or communication at its address set out in this Agreement or another address as specified by that party by wr itten notice to the other.
perturbation.changed_text: 11.1 A notice or other communication under or in connection with th is Agreement shall be in writing, in Eng lish and shall be delivered personally or sent by carrier pigeon, to the party due to receive the no tice or communication at its address set out in this Agreement or another address as specified by that party by wr itten notice to the other.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the acceptable method of notice from fax to carrier pigeon makes the method of notice unreasonable and unreliable, and as a result, unenforceable. This may contradict standards for proper notice under contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Principles of Contract Law - Reasonableness
perturbation.law_citation: Principles of European Contract Law (PECL), Article 1:302
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1:302 of the Principles of European Contract Law states that "Each party owes to the other a duty to co-operate in order to give full effect to the contract". Using an unreliable method of communication, such as a carrier pigeon, violates the duty to co-operate and could render contractual notices ineffective, particularly when more modern methods of communication are readily available and reasonable. This is not a US statute because the agreement is governed by the laws of England and Wales. The PECL is commonly used to interpret European contract law.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies 'fax' as a method of communication, whereas the changed text replaces 'fax' with 'carrier pigeon'. The Law Explanation states that using an unreliable method of communication violates the duty to co-operate and could render contractual notices ineffective. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is empty, Snippet 2 from the Code of Virginia implies the importance of legally sound contracts. The change to 'carrier pigeon' introduces an unreliable method of communication that undermines the contract's effectiveness, thereby creating a legal contradiction as it renders the contract practically unenforceable due to the unreliability of the method, thereby hindering cooperation.

perturbation.original_text: 12 Governing law and jurisdiction The construction, validity and performance of this Agreement sh all be governed by the laws of England and Wales and the part ies hereby submit to the non-exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of England and Wales.
perturbation.changed_text: 12 Governing law and jurisdiction The construction, validity and performance of this Agreement sh all be governed by the laws of North Korea and the part ies hereby submit to the non-exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of England and Wales.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to North Korea introduces a significant legal contradiction. North Korean law is largely inaccessible, unpredictable, and incompatible with the legal framework expected in international commercial agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 addresses the enforceability of choice-of-law provisions. While parties generally have the autonomy to choose the governing law, a court may decline to apply the chosen law if it is contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state in the determination of the particular issue, and which would be the state of the applicable law in the absence of an effective choice of law by the parties. Because North Korean law lacks transparency and due process, it would be contrary to fundamental policies of most nations and would be unenforceable. I have added a US explanation and backup URL because the original contract did not specify a jurisdiction but now specifies jurisdiction in England and Wales.
perturbation.justification: The change in governing law to North Korea introduces a significant legal contradiction because North Korean law is largely inaccessible, unpredictable, and incompatible with the legal framework expected in international commercial agreements. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that because North Korean law lacks transparency and due process, it would be contrary to fundamental policies of most nations and would be unenforceable. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where a contradiction arises when a contract sets a requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal. The original text specified England and Wales, providing a legally recognized and accessible system, while the changed text introduces a system that is likely unenforceable due to its incompatibility with international legal standards.

perturbation.original_text: CONFIDENTIAL Mutual Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 5 7 Duration of obligations The obligations and responsibilities of the parties shall survive the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: CONFIDENTIAL Mutual Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 5 7 Duration of obligations The obligations and responsibilities of the parties shall terminate immediately with the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Stating that the obligations and responsibilities terminate immediately upon termination of the agreement contradicts the purpose of an NDA, which is to protect confidential information even after the agreement ends. This conflicts with the common law principle regarding the protection of trade secrets and confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law Protection of Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Third) of Unfair Competition § 41 recognizes that trade secrets can be protected as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. Eliminating all obligations upon termination of the agreement would undermine the protection of trade secrets, as it would allow the receiving party to freely use or disclose the confidential information immediately after termination, thereby destroying its secrecy and value.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: trade secret | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you trade secret The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) is a piece of legislation created by the Uniform Law Commission (ULC) . The UTSA defines trade secrets and describes claims related to trade secrets. As of 2024, 48 states , the District of Columbia, the U.S. Virgin Islands, and Puerto Rico, have adopted the UTSA. What is a Trade Secret UTSA's Definition The UTSA defines a " trade secret " as: Information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. Non-UTSA Definition Prior to the development of the UTSA, improper use or disclosure of a trade secret was traditionally a common law tort . Sections 757 and 758 of the Restatement of Torts (1939) set forth the basic principles of trade secret law that were widely adopted by U.S. courts . In particular, §757, comment b, listed six factors to be considered in determining whether information constitutes a trade secret: The extent to which the information is known outside the claimant's business. The extent to which it is known by employees and others involved in the business. The extent of measures taken by the claimant to guard the secrecy of the information. The value of the information to the business and its competitors. The amount of effort or money expended by the business in developing the information. The ease or difficulty with which the information could be properly acquired or duplicated by others. Elements of a Trade Secret Claim There are typically three essential elements to a trade secret claim: The subject matter involved must qualify for trade secret protection (see "Scope" below for more on this). The holder of the subject matter must establish that reasonable precautions were taken to prevent disclosure of the subject matter. The trade secret holder must prove that the information was misappropriated or wrongfully taken. Use of a trade secret belonging to another does not always constitute misappropriation. There are two basic situations in which obtaining the use of a trade secret is illegal; where it is acquired through improper means, or where it involves a breach of confidence. Trade secrets may be obtained by lawful means such as independent discovery, reverse engineering, and inadvertent disclosure resulting from the trade secret holder's failure to take reasonable protective measures. The misappropriation of trade secrets is considered a form of unfair competition , and is discussed in the Restatement (Third) of Unfair Competition . Misappropriation of Trade Secrets as a Crime In some circumstances, misappropriation of trade secrets is not only a tort ; it is a federal crime . Economic Espionage Act In 1996, Congress enacted the Economic Espionage Act ("EEA"), codified as 18 U.S.C. §§1831-1839 . In United States v. Sazonov , the defendant Sazonov has been charged with violating the EEA for "attempt[ing] to steal valuable proprietary computer code that took his employer years to develop . . . Sazonov was employed for thirteen years by Susquehanna International Group ("Susquehanna"), a financial services firm headquartered in Pennsylvania with offices in Manhattan." Scope of Subject Matter Customer Lists Customer lists and other lists related to customer business qualify for trade secret protection if the lists' information cannot be ascertained from other generally available sources. In Morlife Inc. v. Perry, 56 Cal. App. 4th 1514 (1997) , the California Court held that customer identities from an organization's list are protected as trade secrets if the identities are not generally kno
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that all obligations terminate immediately upon termination of the agreement. This directly contradicts the purpose of an NDA, which is to protect confidential information even after the agreement ends. The UTSA definition of a trade secret requires 'efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy.' Allowing immediate termination of obligations would undermine these efforts, as it allows the receiving party to freely use or disclose confidential information immediately after termination, destroying its secrecy and value. This falls under Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction because the termination clause sets a deadline (immediate termination) that violates the principle of protecting trade secrets, making the contractual terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 3 Maintaining confidentiality Each party shall, in relation to the Conf idential Information of the other party, exercise in relation to the Confidential Information no lesser security measures and degree of care than those wh ich it applies to its own confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: 3 Maintaining confidentiality Each party shall, in relation to the Conf idential Information of the other party, exercise in relation to the Confidential Information no security measures and zero degree of care whatsoever.
perturbation.explanation: Removing any requirement for security measures or care in protecting confidential information directly contradicts the fundamental purpose of a non-disclosure agreement. This undermines the legal basis for enforcing confidentiality and protecting trade secrets.
perturbation.law_citation: Coco v A N Clark (Engineers) Ltd [1969] RPC 41
perturbation.law_explanation: The case of Coco v A N Clark (Engineers) Ltd [1969] RPC 41 establishes the elements necessary to establish a breach of confidence, including that the information must have been imparted in circumstances importing an obligation of confidence. Removing any obligation to maintain confidentiality or exercise care negates this element, making it impossible to enforce the agreement and protect confidential information under English law, where this agreement is expected to be enforced.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Protheroe v Protheroe [1968] EWCA Civ 7 (01 February 1968) [ Home ] [ Databases ] [ World Law ] [ Multidatabase Search ] [ Help ] [ Feedback ] [ DONATE ] England and Wales Court of Appeal (Civil Division) Decisions You are here: BAILII >> Databases >> England and Wales Court of Appeal (Civil Division) Decisions >> Protheroe v Protheroe [1968] EWCA Civ 7 (01 February 1968) URL: https://www.bailii.org/ew/cases/EWCA/Civ/1968/7.html Cite as: [1968] 1 WLR 519, [1968] 1 All ER 1111, [1968] EWCA Civ 7, [1968] WLR 519, (1968) 19 P & CR 396 [ New search ] [Buy ICLR report: [1968] 1 WLR 519 ] [ Help ] JISCBAILII_CASE_TRUSTS BAILII Citation Number: [1968] EWCA Civ 7 Case No. IN THE SUPREME COURT OF JUDICATURE COURT OF APPEAL Civil Division From Mr Registrar Caird Royal Courts of Justice 1st February. 1968 B e f o r e : THE MASTER OF THE ROLLS (Lord Denning) LORD JUSTICE DANCKWERTS and LORD JUSTICE WIDGERY ____________________ IRENE HILDA PROTHEROE Applicant/Respondent WILLIAM PROTHEROE Respondent/Appellant ____________________ (Transcript from the Shorthand Notes of The Association of Official Shorthandwriters, Ltd., Room 392, Royal Courts of Justice, and 2, New Square, Lincoln's Inn, London, W.C.2.) ____________________ MR KENNETH WHEELER (instructed by Mr R.W.Piatt) appeared as Counsel for the Appellant, MR HAROLD LAW (instructed by Messrs Sowman, Wells & Potter) appeared as Counsel for the Respondent. ____________________ HTML VERSION OF JUDGMENT ____________________ Crown Copyright © THE MASTER OF THE ROLLS ; This case raises another short interesting point. Husband and wife were married on the 20th October, 1954. The wife had a child by a previous marriage. She had two children by this marriage, one born in 1955, a girl, and the other born in 1961, a boy. They bought a house, 151 Faraday Road, Wimbledon as their matrimonial home. It was a leasehold which at that time had 44 years to run and it cost £1,375. It was taken in the husband's name but the wife paid the deposit and the husband paid the expenses. He paid also the Building Society instalments. The wife did not go out to work, but the Registrar was satisfied that they were entitled to the leasehold in equal shares. That is not disputed. In March 1964 the husband left the house. The wife filed a petition for divorce on the ground of the husband's cruelty. That petition was taken out on the 20th July, 1964. There was a decree nisi on the 17th March, 1966, and a decree absolute on the 3rd March, 1967. Here is the important point. After the wife filed her petition, in October 1964 the husband purchased the freehold of the house. The owners asked £500 but he managed to get it down to £200, and eventually he bought the freehold for £200. He borrowed the money from the Building Society. Re paid an extra sum of £3.8s.0d . a month in respect of that freehold interest. In point of law the leasehold merged in the freehold and he was the owner of the whole freehold. The question now arises: What about the wife's half share? It has been contended on behalf of the husband that she only had a half share in the leasehold and that she had no share in the freehold which it was said was entirely his own property. He bought the property after she left. Re had no intention whatever of giving this freehold reversion to her. The point is of considerable financial importance. A valuation has been made of the property. The leasehold would be worth separately £2,450, whereas if the whole were sold freehold, it would be worth £3,950. In other words, £1,500 difference. The husband says that £1,500 difference belongs to him and he had no intention whatever that it should belong to the wife. The short answer to the husband's contention is this: Although the house was in the husband's name, he was a trustee of it for both. It was a family asset which the husband and wife owned in equal shares. Being a trustee, he had an especial advantage in getting the freehold. There is a long established rule of equity f
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly removes any requirement for security measures or care in protecting confidential information, which directly contradicts the fundamental purpose of a non-disclosure agreement. The Law Explanation mentions Coco v A N Clark (Engineers) Ltd [1969] RPC 41, which establishes the need for an obligation of confidence. The modified text eliminates any such obligation, rendering the agreement unenforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 2, Protheroe v Protheroe, is not directly relevant to confidentiality but discusses trust and obligations, indirectly supporting the principle that removing obligations undermines a legal agreement. The modification negates the element of obligation, creating a clear contradiction.
